questionText: What is the difference between a Docker image and a container, and how do they relate to each other?
referenceAnswer: An image is a read-only template that contains everything needed to run an application — the OS base, dependencies, code, and configuration. A container is a running instance of an image, like a process created from that template. You can run multiple containers from the same image, and each container has its own writable layer on top of the read-only image layers. A common misconception is thinking that changes made inside a container modify the image — they don't. When the container is removed, those changes are lost unless you explicitly committed them to a new image or used a volume.
keyPoints: an image is a read-only template, a container is a running instance
multiple containers can run from the same image
changes inside a container do not modify the original image
container data is lost on removal unless persisted with volumes

questionText: You run docker run with your image, but the container exits immediately. What are the most common reasons this happens?
referenceAnswer: A container runs as long as its main process is alive. If the main process finishes or crashes, the container stops. The most common reasons are: the Dockerfile's CMD or ENTRYPOINT runs a command that completes instantly (like echo or a script with no long-running process), the application crashes on startup due to a missing environment variable or config error, or the process runs in the background (daemonizes) and the foreground process exits. To debug, check the logs with docker logs <container> and the exit code. For a web server, make sure it runs in the foreground — for example, node server.js rather than a background daemon.
keyPoints: a container lives only as long as its main process
CMD that finishes instantly causes the container to stop
application crashes on startup cause immediate exit
check docker logs and exit code to diagnose

questionText: Your app runs on port 3000 inside a Docker container, but you can't reach it from your browser at localhost:3000. What is likely missing?
referenceAnswer: By default, container ports are not exposed to the host machine. You need to map the container's port to a host port using the -p flag: docker run -p 3000:3000 maps host port 3000 to container port 3000. The left side is the host port, the right side is the container port — they don't have to match. Another common cause is the app inside the container listening on 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0 — inside a container, 127.0.0.1 only accepts connections from within the container itself. The fix is to bind the app to 0.0.0.0 so it accepts connections from the host through the port mapping.
keyPoints: container ports are not exposed to the host by default
-p host:container maps ports between host and container
app must listen on 0.0.0.0, not 127.0.0.1, inside the container
host port and container port do not have to be the same number

questionText: You store uploaded files inside a Docker container, but they disappear every time the container is restarted. Why, and how do you fix it?
referenceAnswer: Container filesystems are ephemeral — when a container is removed and recreated, all data written inside it is gone. To persist data across restarts, use a Docker volume or a bind mount. A volume is managed by Docker and stored outside the container's filesystem: docker run -v mydata:/app/uploads maps a named volume to the container path. A bind mount maps a host directory directly: -v ./uploads:/app/uploads. Volumes are the recommended approach for data that needs to survive container lifecycle. A common mistake is storing important data (like database files or uploads) inside the container without a volume and losing everything on the next docker-compose down.
keyPoints: container filesystems are ephemeral — data is lost on removal
volumes persist data outside the container's filesystem
bind mounts map a host directory into the container
always use volumes for data that must survive container restarts

questionText: You change your application code, but the running container still serves the old version. What did you forget, and how does Docker's build cache affect this?
referenceAnswer: Changing your source code on the host does not automatically update the code inside a running container — unless you have a bind mount pointing to the source directory. If you're using a Dockerfile that copies code with COPY . ., you need to rebuild the image with docker build and then recreate the container. Docker's build cache reuses layers that haven't changed: if your COPY instruction is after a layer that changed, it and all subsequent layers are rebuilt. A common optimization is to copy package.json and install dependencies before copying the rest of the code, so dependency installation is cached unless package.json changes. Without this, every code change triggers a full dependency reinstall.
keyPoints: code changes on the host don't appear in the container without rebuild or bind mount
rebuild the image and recreate the container after code changes
Docker caches layers and rebuilds from the first changed layer onward
copy package.json first to cache dependency installation
